iterations.goal: Extract key information about Shannon's life and contributions
iterations.answer: Shannon's key contributions: (1) 1948 paper 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication'; (2) entropy as measure of information; (3) channel capacity theorem; (4) coined the term 'bit'
Claude Elwood Shannon (1916–2001) was an American mathematician and electrical engineer known as the father of information theory...

final_answer: Claude Elwood Shannon (1916–2001) was an American mathematician and electrical engineer widely regarded as the father of information theory. His landmark 1948 paper 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication' introduced the concept of entropy as a mathematical measure of information content, defined in bits: H(X) = -Σ p(xᵢ) log₂ p(xᵢ). His most important contribution is the channel capacity theorem, which proves a fundamental limit C = B log₂(1+S/N) on reliable information transmission — a result that underlies all of modern digital communications, the Internet, mobile telephony, and data storage. Shannon also coined the term 'bit' (binary digit), proved that one-time pads are the only unconditionally secure cipher, and contributed to chess programming and artificial intelligence. He spent most of his career at Bell Labs and MIT, where he was known for riding a unicycle while juggling through the lab corridors.
